title: 𒋳𒈠 𒀀𒉿𒈝
titleEn: Laws of Hammurabi — §26–41: the soldier and his fief (ix 66 – xii 62)
part: Laws §26–41 · the soldier & his fief (Col. ix 66 – xii 62)
draftNote: The § chips number the laws (Harper’s sectioning, still standard); long laws span several tap-units. Readings checked sign-by-sign against Harper’s 1904 edition.
source: Louvre stele, after R.F. Harper, The Code of Hammurabi, King of Babylon (Chicago, 1904): transliteration pp. 18–25, autograph Plates XIV–XIX, List of Signs. Readings are Harper’s in modern subscript notation. Cuneiform in Old Babylonian monumental sign forms via the SantakkuM webfont by Sylvie Vanséveren (CC BY 4.0); pronunciation: conventional reconstructed Old Babylonian. Fresh translations made directly from the Akkadian; Harper’s public-domain translation consulted for sense only.
about: Sixteen laws on the men who hold land from the crown in exchange for service — the rēdûm (Harper’s rid ṣâbê) and the fisher bāʾirum. Dodge the levy and your informer takes your house (§26); a prisoner of war gets his fief back, a deserter loses his in three years (§27–31); a captive is ransomed up a ladder — own house, city temple, palace — but never with the fief itself (§32). Officers who fleece their own men die for it (§33–34). Then the wall around service land: crown livestock and fief-land cannot be bought at any price (§35–38), only purchased property may be deeded or pledged (§39), and the one exception (§40) carries the duty with it. §41’s barter trick fails: the soldier keeps land and cash both.

sentences.n: Desertion by proxy. The hireling ({amēlu}agram — the sign Harper’s own list identifies as agru “hired man,” with the person-determinative) is not punished; the man who dodged the levy dies, and the munaggirum — the informer who denounced him — is paid with the dead man’s estate. Ancient whistle-blower economics.

sentences.n: dannat šarrim, “the stronghold of the king,” is where campaigns go wrong: Harper renders it “fortress,” and the man is turru — taken, a prisoner of war. The state does not let his fief lie idle; land and duty pass together to a substitute. The [ki] in wa-ar-[ki]-šu is Harper’s bracket: the sign is damaged on the stele and restored. Note ilkum, the fief-duty — the keyword of this whole block: land is held from the crown in exchange for service, and the two are never separated.

sentences.n: Succession in the ranks: the fief is heritable — but only with the service attached. il-kam and a-la-kam rhyme on the stele: the duty (ilkum) and the going (alākum) are one package. Harper’s brackets in i-li-[ik] mark another damaged sign, restored from the parallel phrases all around it.

sentences.n: The widow’s portion: šaluštum, one-third — not inheritance but maintenance, sized to bring the boy up until he can serve. The crown keeps the other two-thirds working under whoever holds the duty meanwhile.

sentences.n: The deserter proper. uddappir — “he made himself scarce” — is the verb for going off-grid. ina pani ilkim, literally “in the face of the duty”: he fled *because of* it. The year-count is written MU III{kam} — the year-sign, three wedges, and the ordinal marker KAM you met in §13.

sentences.n: The mirror of §27: the prisoner gets everything back; the deserter, nothing. Three years of another man’s service extinguish his claim. i-ir-ri-iš is erēšum, “to demand, to claim” (the same verb as “to cultivate” — he wants his land worked and his name on it; the code says no).

sentences.n: The ransom chain begins. tamkārum, the merchant, fronts the prisoner’s ransom money abroad — here spelled tam-KAR; at §40 the same word is written with the signs DAM.GÀR. ip-ṭu-ra-aš-šu is paṭārum, “to release, redeem”: the verb this law turns on, four times over.

sentences.n: Rung two and three of the ladder: first the man’s own estate, then bīt ili ālišu — “the house of the god of his city,” the temple treasury as community ransom-fund. Harper writes the verb padārum where modern editions read paṭārum — the plain and emphatic t are hard to tell apart in this spelling.

sentences.n: The ladder tops out at the crown: no Babylonian soldier is left in enemy hands for want of money. And the last clause is the point of the whole block — the fief is untouchable. The stele has one more carver’s slip here: i-pa-ad-da-RI carved where -AR was meant; Harper prints ri(=ar), correcting the stone in parentheses as he did at §25.

sentences.n: Now the officers. PA.PA and NU.TUR are the sign-names Harper keeps: modern editions read them ša ḫaṭṭātim (“he of the staves,” a captain) and laputtûm (lieutenant). ṣāb nisḫātim — “troops of removal,” men struck from the rolls — is the padding of muster-lists with names that will never fight. Here ḫarrān is carved with the single road-sign KASKAL, where §26 spelled it out ḫar-ra-an: same word, two writings, six columns apart.

sentences.n: The catalogue of garrison abuse, one count per line: theft of the man’s kit, extortion (ḫabālum — the same “wronging” the prologue vowed the strong would not do to the weak), and renting out your own troops as farmhands.

sentences.n: The list ends with the king’s gift — campaign rewards stolen by the chain of command — and the officer dies for it. The stele stumbles a third time on the same word: il-te-DI carved for il-te-ḳi (“he took”), the DI sign again where ḳi belongs, exactly as in §25 and here corrected by Harper as (=ḳi). The [a] is his bracket: a damaged sign restored.

sentences.n: Army livestock is crown property: buy it and you have bought nothing — the silver is simply gone. LID.GUD.ZUN is Harper’s reading of the cattle-signs (the cow AB₂, the ox GU₄, and the plural marker the stele writes ḪI+A); ṣênê is the flock-word over the sheep-sign LU. i-na ga-ti — “from the hand of” — is the phrase for a direct, no-questions-asked purchase; caveat emptor was not a defense in Babylon.

sentences.n: Five lines, no šumma — a flat rule, not a case. The nāši biltim, “bearer of the yield,” is the state tenant who pays his rent in produce; his holding, like the soldier’s, is crown land and cannot be turned into cash. §§36–38 wall the fief off from the market; §40 will cut the one exception.

sentences.n: The sanction behind §36. The sale tablet — ṭuppum, the clay deed itself — is physically broken: no document, no sale. The buyer loses the silver too; the land walks back to the fief-holder by the next law. Note biltim here carved with the single tribute-sign GUN, where §36 spelled it bi-il-tim — the carvers alternate freely.

sentences.n: The wall has an inner face too: the holder himself cannot alienate the fief — not to family by testament (šaṭārum, “to write over”), not to creditors for an iʾiltum, a debt-bond. Service land must stay whole under the man who serves.

sentences.n: The counterweight to §38, word for word: what a man purchases with his own silver is fully his — heritable, giftable, mortgageable. The code is drawing the oldest line in property law: tenure of office versus private estate.

sentences.n: The one gate in the wall. Some holdings may change hands — but the ilkum travels with the land, never separating from it. The first word is the law’s crux: the stele has the single woman-sign SAL, which Harper reads aššatum “wife” with a question mark in his own sign-list; modern editions take the holder here to be the nadītum, the cloistered priestess-businesswoman who appears throughout the code. Speculation flagged on both sides — the sign is one wedge short of certainty either way.

sentences.n: The clever workaround: not a sale (§36 forbade that) but a swap — pûḫum, exchange, the same root as the substitute (pūḫšu) of §26 — with niplātim, balancing payments, to sweeten it. The code has seen this trick before it was tried.

sentences.n: The block’s dry punchline: the swap unwinds one way only. The fief-holder gets his land back and pockets the niplātim; the schemer loses both. With that, the soldier’s laws close — §42 turns to the tenant farmer who lets his field lie idle.

glossary.šumma: if — the word that opens every law of the code
glossary.lū: or; either (lū … lū “whether … or”); with verb: “let …”
glossary.rēdûm: the soldier — Harper reads the sign-pair AGA₃.ÚS as rid ṣâbê “follower of the troops”; the regular fief-holding infantryman of the code
glossary.bāʾirum: the fisher (written ŠU.ḪA, “hand + fish”) — a militia class holding a fief on the same terms as the soldier
glossary.ḫarrānum: road; campaign: ḫarrān šarrim “the king’s campaign” (spelled ḫar-ra-an, or carved with the road-sign KASKAL)
glossary.šarrum: king: šarrum “the king”, šar-ri-im “of the king”
glossary.qabûm: to speak, say: iqbû, iqtabi, iqabbûma
glossary.agrum: hired man, hireling: {amēlu}agram “a hireling” (the person-determinative + the sign Harper’s list reads agru)
glossary.pūḫum: substitute; exchange: pūḫšu “his substitute” (§26), upīḫ “he took in exchange” (§41)
glossary.ṭarādum: to send (off): iddarad “he sent (as his substitute)” — Harper’s reading of iṭṭarad
glossary.dâkum: to kill; N-stem iddâk “he shall be killed” — the code’s death sentence
glossary.munaggirum: the denouncer, informer: munaggiršu “his denouncer shall take his house”
glossary.tabālum: to take away, carry off: itabbal
glossary.dannatum: stronghold, fortress; the hard place: ina dannat šarrim turru “taken while in the king’s fortress” — captured on campaign
glossary.târum-D: D-stem of târum: to give back: turru “taken away/captured”; utarrūšumma “they shall return (it) to him”
glossary.warki: after, behind: warkišu “after him, in his absence”
glossary.eqlum: field (written A.ŠÀ): eqelšu “his field” — the fief-land this whole block protects
glossary.kirûm: orchard, garden (written {giš}SAR, the KIRI₆ sign): kirāšu “his orchard”
glossary.šanûm: another, a second one: ana šanîm “to another”, šanûm “another man”
glossary.nadānum: to give, pay: inaddin, iddinam, iddinušum; nadānim “to give”
glossary.ilkum: the fief-duty — service owed to the crown for land held: ilikšu illak “he shall perform his duty”; the keyword of §§26–41
glossary.târum: to return: ul itârma “he shall never again …”
glossary.ālum: city (URU): ālum u rabiānum “the city and the mayor”
glossary.kašādum: to reach, overcome: iktašassu “(the River) has overcome him”
glossary.-ma: and (clause-chaining particle); also “it is X that…”
glossary.mārum: son (DUMU) — here: a minor, not yet able to sell
glossary.leʾûm: to be able: ileʾʾi “he is able” (Harper: i-li-i)
glossary.nadānum-N: N-stem of nadānum: to be given: innaddin “it shall be given”, innaddiššumma “it shall be given to him”
glossary.ṣeḫērum: to be small, young: ṣeḫirma “he is (too) young, and”
glossary.šaluštum: one-third: šalušti eqlim “a third of the field” — the widow’s child-raising portion
glossary.ummum: mother: ana ummišu “to his mother”, ummašu urabbāšu “his mother shall raise him”
glossary.rabûm-D: D-stem of rabûm: to bring up, raise: urabbāšu “she shall raise him”
glossary.pānum: front, face: ina pani “in front of”
glossary.nadûm: to cast, throw (a charge; a body into the river): iddi, nadû, iddû
glossary.duppurum: to go off, abscond: uddappir “he made himself scarce” — the deserter’s verb
glossary.3-kam: the 3rd; MU III{kam} “the third year” (KAM marks ordinals, as in §13)
glossary.erēšum: to ask, demand; also to cultivate: irriš “he demands (his field back)”
glossary.tamkārum: merchant (written tam-KAR §32, DAM.GÀR §40): the ransom-fronting, land-buying businessman of the code
glossary.paṭārum: to loosen, redeem, ransom (Harper spells padārum): ipṭuraššu “he ransomed him”, ipaddar “he shall redeem”
glossary.kašādum-Š: Š-stem of kašādum: to cause to reach: uštakšidaššu “he got him back (to his city)”
glossary.ramānum: self: ramānšu ipaddar “he shall redeem himself”
glossary.ilum: god; here: the temple as owner
glossary.paṭārum-N: N-stem: to be redeemed: ippaddar “he shall be ransomed”
glossary.ekallum: palace (É.GAL “great house”) — the crown as owner
glossary.PA.PA: an officer — Harper keeps the sign-name PA.PA; modern editions read ša ḫaṭṭātim, “he of the staves”: the captain
glossary.NU.TUR: an officer — Harper keeps NU.TUR; modern editions read laputtûm: the lieutenant
glossary.ṣābum: troops, soldiery (the sign ERIM): ṣāb nisḫātim “struck-off troops”
glossary.nisḫātum: removal, the struck-off: ṣāb nisḫātim — men removed from the muster-rolls, phantom soldiers
glossary.rašûm: to get, acquire: irtaši “he has acquired”, iraššû “he acquires”
glossary.redûm: to lead, conduct; bring back: irdiam “he produced”, irtediaššu “he has led him back”, ireddišu “he shall conduct him”
glossary.numātum: goods, household property: numāt bēl bītim “the goods of the house’s owner”
glossary.ḫabālum: to wrong, oppress: iḫtabal “he has oppressed (a soldier)” — the very verb of the prologue’s promise
glossary.igrum: hire, wages: ana igrim ittadin “he hired (him) out”
glossary.dīnum: case, lawsuit; judgment: dīn napištim “a case of life”
glossary.dannum: the strong one: ina dīnim ana dannim ištarak “in a lawsuit he handed (him) to the strong”
glossary.šarākum: to give away, hand over: ištarak
glossary.qīštum: gift: qīšti šarrum ana rēdîm iddinu “the gift the king gave the soldier”
glossary.awīlum: man; the free citizen, the code’s first legal class
glossary.liātum: cattle: Harper’s LID.GUD.ZUN — the cow-sign, the ox-sign, and the plural marker (the stele carves ḪI+A)
glossary.ṣēnum: sheep, the flock: ṣēnē over the sheep-sign LU
glossary.qātum: hand: ina qāt “from the hand of”
glossary.šâmum: to buy: ištām “he bought”, išammu “he buys”
glossary.kaspum: silver — the code’s money, written KÙ.BABBAR “shining precious-metal”
glossary.elûm: to go up; here: to forfeit: ina kaspišu ītelli “he goes up out of his silver” — his money is gone
glossary.našûm: to bear, carry: ittanašši “he shall bear (the penalty)”
glossary.biltum: yield, tribute, rent: nāši biltim “bearer of the yield”, the state tenant (spelled bi-il-tim, or carved with the tribute-sign GUN)
glossary.ṭuppum: the clay tablet; the deed: ṭuppašu iḫḫeppi “his tablet shall be smashed” — the sale annulled physically
glossary.ḫepûm: to break, smash: iḫḫeppi “it shall be broken”
glossary.bēlum: owner, lord: bēl ḫulqim “owner of the lost property”
glossary.šaṭārum: to write; to assign in writing, deed over (Harper: išaddar): ul išaddar “he shall not deed”
glossary.iʾiltum: bond, debt-obligation: ana iʾiltišu / eḫiltišu “for his debt”
glossary.aššatum-SAL: Harper’s reading of the woman-sign SAL in §40 — aššatum “wife”, with his own question mark; modern editions read nadītum, the cloistered priestess who buys and sells throughout the code. The sign is one wedge short of certainty either way.
glossary.aḫûm: outside, other: ilkum aḫûm “another (kind of) tenure”
glossary.pûḫum: to exchange, barter: upīḫ “he took in exchange” (Harper: u-bi-iḫ)
